item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has a few tone issues and a slight LOLFoundation. The idea is not the most interesting of all time, but it is interesting enough; could even work as a Thaumiel (in my opinion).
No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just goes to show that an SCP doesn't need to be reality ending or *Super super scary* to be good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bifurcation?

item.title: Re: A nit to pick.
item.content:encoded: Wish you could +1 comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If one of the signs is removed from the door frame while an object or individual was "straddling" between the two location what would occur?

item.title: A nit to pick.
item.content:encoded:
This is a well written and obviously popular article but…
The phrase "exiting out" or "leaving out" is popular usage but it is redundant.
If there is a door somewhere you can exit into that needs to be in containment too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image in this article was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact anqxyr or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It should probably read

Addendum: Due to its Safe-class nature and usefulness, all instances of SCP-1021 have been set up in various Foundation sites to act as an emergency exit, and the ███████ Cinema has been renovated to act as an evacuation command area where Foundation staff may seek refuge. The evacuation points in all sites must be kept in a clear area guarded by level 3 security staff, in order to prevent its misuse as a method of smuggling SCPs out of their home sites.

List of SCP-1021 assigned locations and corresponding door.

instead. It's pretty clear that this was added later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps the article should be revised to include something about procedures to remotely disable/destroy the signs in case the connecting site is compromised. Even better, a mechanical way to force the doors at the cinema shut that would prevent the signs from functioning would fix this flaw as well as be able to prevent an "ocean flooding" incident like in SCP-249. Two of the signs are on ships, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you misunderstood. The signs only work in one direction, leading from the Sites to the cinema. The guards at the cinema are stationed there to maintain the area as an evacuation site and to keep anybody from leaving the sites that shouldn't be. If anything, Notoriety has a good point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-970 - The Recursive Room

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done, nothing huge but it makes good use of the space it takes up! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really don't like how they listed each Foundation Site that an exit sign can be found at - Out of ALL the things that could be redacted, something that could help assist with smuggling out SCPs should be one of the redacted things. Also, is the location they lead to fully secured, in case something DOES get past the guards?

-1 for sloppiness from the SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Which SCP is this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How about the most important one of all: impossible. Eh? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very good. Only thing is the tense suddenly changes here to future, even though its made clear the cinema has already been established as a safe house;

security detail are to remain for 1 month and secure the building.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All instances of SCP-1021 are currently in use in various sites and facilities

SCP-1021 is a set of 7 EXIT signs designed in Art Moderne dating back to the 1940's. The anomalous effect of SCP-1021 will only manifest if it is placed directly over a door made of solid wood

Due to its Safe-class nature and usefulness, all instances of SCP-1021 have been set up in various Foundation sites to act as an emergency exit

I don't personally like the idea of the Foundation making use of an object because of it's "usefulness." It doesn't seem to characterize the Foundation very well to me. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
freaky and doesn't try to kill you.
my kind of SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is why I gave it a +1. I like seeing the Foundation contain stuff that's just odd, and nothing more than odd; I like items that are a little useful, but not enough for wish fulfillment fantasies. It's a good change of pace from "The world is hosed and the Foundation is just slowing that down."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like that the last paragraph sort of implies a disaster just waiting to happen. The Foundation sees the usefulness and also sees the risk of smugglers, but readers can easily connect these two points: If shit really hits the fan, all sorts of dangerous organisms now have a direct gate to Michigan, regardless of how secure the area around their home facilities is. You could totally believe that this is overlooked by the people writing the report or just willfully ignored.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice idea with the name of the company buying the signs; how exactly one recognizes the exit? Do you see the exit point when you open the door, or does it look like the usual place?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made some minor grammatical changes and reworded some things (i.e. "seek refuge in times of peril," seemed a little too dramatically-worded for a scientific document). Well done, OP—good example of an SCP being believably used as a tool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is surprisingly neat. Well done, and +1'ed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: except we've already got one like that. It's a hallway, you go out the north door and you're immediately at the south door… except that if you do this a few hundred times in a row, you'll start to notice differences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or then it could be argued that the signs don't teleport you, but place you in a consistent location in an identical alternate universe… O.o

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you would just walk into yourself. But if you both pressed yourselves to your respective right, you would get through the door to where you were in the first place.
It would be kind of freaky but not really, because you wouldn't even be able to talk to yourself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would guess that you'd walk right through it. Unless you mean put the exit sign on the outside of the door… In which case google "divide by zero" and click images.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was the part that pushed it into the +1 category for me ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like to think the [ACCESS DENIED] is the ladies' room.
Also, very good points.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kind of want the signs all placed on the seven doors in the theater so that you can't get out without breaking a wall. With that said, what would happen if you put an exit sign on the door that it corresponds to?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If they were, why would they allow doors to Site 19, Site 4, two SCPS ships, and even an [ACCESS DENIED] to be in such a low security area?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't comment very often, but I am upvoting this because I really like how the artifact is just…odd. It's kind of nice in a directory where most things are creepy, fear-inducing, or downright murderous to have the occasional artifact that just Does A Strange Thing for Reasons Unknown. It's just weird enough to need containment, but also just weird enough to be useful in times of emergency. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Of course. Silly me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Of course they're one-way. They're EXIT signs, not EXIT-AND-ENTRANCE signs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a neat little item that is potentially useful, without really crossing the line into "I want it," not because it's dangerous, but because … well, why would I want it?
One question: are the door connections one-way? That is, if I put one of these on my closet door, and walk through, can I walk back through the door I just exited and end up back in my home?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't say I much care for the writing, and we already have a door that connects to other doors somewhere… but I will say that I like "Scenic Cultural Places" as a new foundation front.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That may have been my favorite part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hope people also got the joke with the organization that bought the cinema.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of the Ticket from the Lost Room miniseries. Still, this seems reasonable. Rather low-key, but I'm fine with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this for two reasons. First, I always love very short SCPs, the ones that get to the point and don't add too much fluff. Second, I like it because there aren't dragons or demons or a black hole on the other end. It lets out at a theater in fucking Michigan. It's realistically unrealistic. It does have some tone issues, specifically in the second Description paragraph, but I like it all in all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: seconded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the return from your moon trip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it might add a little to the article to have the table at the end say which door at the cinema each instance comes out of. Not a huge improvement, I know, but a neat detail that would increase the immersion a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. I can't say that I find this interesting, scary, creepy, or any of the other adjectives that would describe a good SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yes please. love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't do a thing for me. It seems too… nice…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to install one at SCP-908.

I should point out that this is basically a less-interesting SCP-120.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one. Not particularly dangerous, but interesting. +1
EDIT: Also, you might want to fix up the title.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. There's nothing unsettling or dangerous about the objects, but there doesn't need to be. They're just plain weird.

If you want to add a sense of subtle horror about it (as many insist upon in SCP objects), consider having a note by a researcher on an anomalous effect that seems minor but the reader can realize is a trend or something else. Having the exit put you at your destination a second or two before or after your entrance time could work (normally, no real effect, but have two people exit within seconds of each other and suddenly you get two bodies in one space).
